name: Gipuzkoan Basque (Gipuzkera)

ancestors.notes: Gipuzkoan is the closest living dialect to Euskara Batua and served as the primary model for standardization; grapheme mappings are nearly identical to the standard.

notes: Gipuzkoan (Gipuzkera). Central Basque, spoken in Gipuzkoa; the closest living dialect to Euskara Batua and its primary reference variety. VALIDATED against the surrounding Illustration-of-the-IPA literature. The three-way sibilant contrast (apical /s̺/ = s, laminal /s̻/ = z, postalveolar /ʃ/ = x) is retained in eastern Gipuzkoan but is variable and neutralised in western Gipuzkoan areas, where the merger characteristic of Western Basque intrudes (Bedialauneta & Hualde 2023: 1098) — hence the base three-way inventory is kept but is not uniform across the province. Aspiration (/h/) is absent: like the rest of western/central Basque, Gipuzkoan lost /h/ and has no aspirated stops (Hualde 2018: 2). The voiceless palatal stop /c/ is merging with the affricate /tʃ/ in westernmost Gipuzkoan towns (Eibar and neighbours; Hualde, Lujanbio & Zubiri 2010: 118). Voiced stops /b d ɡ/ are approximants [β ð ɣ] intervocalically. NOTE: lexically contrastive pitch-accent is a feature of specific northern-Biscayan and Navarrese varieties (e.g. Goizueta; Hualde et al. 2010: 113, 123-124), not of general Gipuzkoan, which has predominantly stress accent; no pitch-accent claim is made for this spec.

sources.author: Hualde, J.I. & Ortiz de Urbina, J. (eds.)
sources.title: A Grammar of Basque
